text: robotic assisted femoral and inguinal hernia repair in a female patient by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a fifty years old female who presented with left groin bulge with on and off pain and discomfort during activity and exercise. This video demonstrates the robotic assisted tap hernia repair with mesh. During the robotic exploration of the left groin, she was found to have left indirect inguinal hernia and left femoral hernia. Both hernias were repaired with placement of a large mesh to cover the both defect. The robotic approach allowed the surgeon to diagnose and repair the femoral hernia in this patient.

A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament to access the preperitoneal space. A preperitoneal flap is created, and the round ligament is separated from it. The surgeon clears preperitoneal fat in the Hasselbach triangle, dissects inferiorly to reveal the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, and identifies a femoral hernia. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the femoral canal is mobilized and removed.

The surgeon continues dissection inferiorly and laterally, identifying the right indirect inguinal defect. The incarcerated preperitoneal fat is completely reduced, revealing a large amount of preperitoneal fat previously trapped in the left indirect inguinal defect.

The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly to complete the preperitoneal pocket. A large 3D mesh is inserted into the space to cover both hernia defects. The mesh is secured to the abdominal wall using a TCO, and the peritoneal defect is closed with an absorbable 2-0 V-Loc suture in a simple running fashion.
